original_hypothesis.title: deployASDRouter.js appears to call ASDRouter.updateWhitelist which does not exist, implying whitelist/config wiring mismatch that can brick cross-chain flow
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The provided deployment script for ASDRouter calls `contract.updateWhitelist(...)` after deploying ASDRouter, but ASDRouter has no such function; whitelist management lives in ASDUSDC. This suggests either (a) the script is stale/wrong, (b) the on-chain deployed router differs from audited source, or (c) intended configuration steps (whitelisting, approvals) are not being executed correctly. Any of these can cause production deployment to be misconfigured and the cross-chain mint pipeline to fail.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code/deployment inconsistency visible in the repo. Misdeployment is a realistic cause of protocol outages and can also create unexpected trust boundary gaps (e.g., whitelisting not set, approvals missing). Phase D should reconcile deployed bytecode, scripts, and expected wiring.
deployment_assumptions: Production deployment uses (or was based on) the provided deployASDRouter.js script or similar automation
No other post-deploy steps exist to correctly configure ASDUSDC whitelist and DEX approvals
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: deployASDRouter.js calls updateWhitelist() on the router, but asdRouter.sol contains no updateWhitelist function; also constructor args count mismatch (script passes 4 args while constructor expects 5).

proof_of_signal.pattern: contractFactory.deploy(addresses.note, lzEndpointEID, addresses.crocSwap, addresses.crocImpact) (missing _asdUSDCAddress); then calls contract.updateWhitelist(...) which ASDRouter does not implement

preconditions: Team relies on these scripts for deployment or they influenced production configuration
No compensating manual configuration was performed

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: deployASDRouter.js lines 28-42 vs asdRouter.sol constructor signature
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: router could be deployed with wrong params or left unconfigured, breaking whitelist/flow
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No on-chain setters exist in ASDRouter to correct config post-deploy

analysis_questions: What exact router bytecode is deployed on target networks and does it match this source?
How is ASDUSDC whitelist set in production (since router cannot set it)?
Is asdUSDC address correctly wired into the router constructor in production deployments?
validation_requirements: Compare deployed router constructor args and storage values to expected addresses
Verify repository scripts used for production vs tests
Ensure ASDUSDC whitelist and DEX approvals are executed in a correct, auditable post-deploy checklist

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not an attacker-driven exploit; rather, a likely operational deployment failure that can render the protocol non-functional (all lzCompose attempts fail whitelist checks or later swap steps).

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users may be refunded in intermediate assets or see failures; cross-chain minting outages
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires redeploy or migration if immutable params are wrong

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No automated check that scripts match constructor ABI / contract methods

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: CI step: compile-time/typechain validation of scripts against ABIs

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
